content: A practical rice portion is about 1/2 cup cooked (around 80 to 100 g), which is roughly 100 to 130 kcal depending on type. For higher activity needs, 1 cup cooked can fit better.
One serving of bread is usually 1 slice (about 25 to 35 g). Choose whole grain bread when possible for more fiber and slower blood sugar rise.
A common pasta serving is about 1 cup cooked (roughly 140 to 160 g). Dry pasta portions are often around 56 g (2 oz) before cooking.
A lean meat serving is about 85 to 100 g cooked, close to the size of a deck of cards. Prefer grilling, baking, or steaming over deep frying.
A fish portion is typically 85 to 120 g cooked. Fatty fish like salmon provide omega-3 fats and are often recommended about 2 times per week.
One fruit serving is usually 1 medium fruit, 1/2 cup chopped fruit, or 1 cup berries. Whole fruit is generally better than juice for fiber and fullness.
A vegetable serving is often 1 cup raw leafy vegetables or 1/2 cup cooked vegetables. Aim for multiple colors across the day for broader micronutrients.
Typical dairy portions are 1 cup milk, 3/4 to 1 cup yogurt, or about 30 g cheese. Pick lower sugar options and watch sodium in processed cheese products.
WHO advises limiting free sugars to less than 10% of daily energy, with benefits at below 5% for extra health gains. For a 2000 kcal diet, 5% is about 25 g added sugar.
Frequent high sugar intake can increase calorie load, trigger blood glucose spikes, and over time raise risk for weight gain, insulin resistance, and dental caries.
General guidance is to stay below 2300 mg sodium per day, and many people with hypertension benefit from a lower target near 1500 mg per day when feasible.
Excess sodium intake can raise blood pressure, especially in salt-sensitive individuals. Reducing sodium often improves blood pressure control within weeks.
Many heart-health guidelines suggest keeping saturated fat below 10% of total calories, and closer to 5 to 6% for people with high cardiovascular risk.
Artificial trans fats should be avoided as much as possible because they increase cardiovascular risk. Check labels for partially hydrogenated oils.
Unsaturated fats from foods like olive oil, nuts, seeds, and fish are generally preferred over saturated and trans fats for heart and metabolic health.
A common adult baseline is about 0.8 g protein per kg body weight daily, with higher needs possible for older adults, athletes, or those in weight-loss phases.
Adults are often encouraged to target around 25 to 38 g fiber daily. Fiber supports gut health, satiety, cholesterol control, and steadier glucose response.
E120 (carmine/cochineal) is a red dye derived from insects. Some consumers avoid it for religious, ethical, or allergy reasons.
E621 (MSG) is a flavor enhancer. It is considered safe by major regulators at normal intake levels, but sodium contribution still matters for sodium-restricted diets.
E150d is a caramel coloring used in dark beverages and sauces. It mainly affects color, not nutritional value, but often appears in ultra-processed foods.
E211 (sodium benzoate) is a preservative. In acidic foods it helps prevent spoilage. Some people prefer limiting highly processed foods that use multiple additives.
E numbers identify approved additives. Not all additives are harmful, but frequent intake of heavily processed foods can displace more nutrient-dense choices.
Gelatin source matters for halal diets. Porcine gelatin is typically not halal, while halal-certified bovine or fish gelatin may be acceptable.
Carmine (E120) and alcohol-containing flavor extracts may be avoided in some halal practices. Certification labels and ingredient source details are important.
Gluten is found in wheat, barley, and rye. People with celiac disease require strict gluten avoidance and need attention to cross-contamination.
Nut allergies can be severe. Check for peanuts and tree nuts, plus precautionary labels like 'may contain'. Cross-contact can happen during manufacturing.
Milk allergy differs from lactose intolerance. Milk allergy involves immune response to proteins like casein/whey, while lactose intolerance is carbohydrate digestion related.
Soy is a common allergen. Ingredient labels may list soybean oil, soy protein, tofu, or soy flour. Some refined soy oils may be tolerated by some individuals.
Egg allergens can appear in baked goods, sauces, dressings, and some processed foods. Label reading is critical for confirmed egg allergy.
Shellfish allergy often includes crustaceans such as shrimp, crab, and lobster. Reactions can be serious; strict avoidance and emergency planning are important.
WHO dietary patterns emphasize vegetables, fruits, legumes, whole grains, and nuts; lower free sugars, salt, and unhealthy fats; and minimal ultra-processed foods.
AHA-aligned eating focuses on fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, fish, and unsaturated fats while limiting sodium, added sugar, and saturated fat.
Adult BMI categories are commonly: underweight <18.5, healthy 18.5 to 24.9, overweight 25.0 to 29.9, obesity >=30. BMI is a screening tool, not a diagnosis.
For diabetes nutrition, emphasize consistent carbohydrate quality, high-fiber foods, lower added sugars, and portion control. Pair carbs with protein/fat to blunt spikes.
For hypertension, DASH-style eating is helpful: more fruits, vegetables, legumes, and low-fat dairy; less sodium, processed meat, and high-salt packaged foods.
NutriScore grades foods from A (more favorable nutrition profile) to E (less favorable), balancing negative nutrients (sugar, saturated fat, sodium, energy) and positive ones (fiber, protein).
Calorie budgeting works best by setting a realistic daily target, distributing intake across meals, and prioritizing high-satiety foods to stay within goals consistently.
Daily fluid needs vary by climate, body size, and activity. A common practical target is around 2 to 3 liters/day from beverages and water-rich foods unless medically restricted.
Vitamin D supports calcium absorption, bone health, and immune function. Sources include sunlight exposure, fortified foods, eggs, fatty fish, and supplements when indicated.
Iron supports oxygen transport. Heme iron from meat is generally absorbed better than non-heme iron from plants; vitamin C helps improve non-heme iron absorption.
Calcium is essential for bones, muscle, and nerve function. Sources include dairy, calcium-set tofu, fortified plant milks, and some leafy greens.
Vitamin B12 supports nerve function and red blood cell production. It is mainly found in animal foods and fortified products, so strict vegans often need supplementation.
Low-fat labels do not automatically mean healthy. Some low-fat products compensate with added sugar, starch, or sodium, so the full nutrition panel still matters.
Sugar-free usually means very little sugar per serving, but total carbs and calories can still be significant. No-added-sugar products may still contain natural sugars.
Natural and organic labels have specific meanings that vary by region. Organic refers to production standards, while natural does not always imply lower calories or sugar.
Healthier swaps include fruit with yogurt instead of candy, nuts or roasted chickpeas instead of chips, and water or unsweetened tea instead of sugary beverages.
A simple meal plan framework is: 1/2 plate non-starchy vegetables, 1/4 lean protein, 1/4 whole grains or starchy foods, plus healthy fats and hydration.
